# Transfer Report: Direct Laser Writing of Liquid-Crystal Photonic Architectures

## Abstract

This transfer report summarises the first year of doctoral work on the
fabrication of three-dimensional photonic elements from polymerizable
liquid-crystal (LC) mesogens using two-photon polymerization (2PP). We
demonstrate that direct laser writing in a nematic reactive mesogen host
permits voxel-level control over the local optical axis, enabling
geometric-phase optics whose retardance profile is sculpted in three
dimensions rather than imposed by a single planar alignment layer.

Preliminary experiments characterise the birefringence of the cross-linked
network (measured Δn ≈ 0.14 at 633 nm) and map the dependence of alignment
fidelity on writing speed, laser power, and cell surface anchoring. A
figure-of-merit is proposed relating diffraction efficiency of fabricated
Pancharatnam-Berry gratings to the achieved director-field uniformity.

## Key Results

1. Stable planar alignment retained after development for line spacings
   down to 0.8 µm, confirmed by polarised optical microscopy.
2. Fabricated polarisation gratings reach 87% first-order diffraction
   efficiency at the design wavelength.
3. A finite-difference time-domain model reproduces the measured angular
   response to within 3%, validating the design pipeline.

## Plan for Confirmation of Status

The next phase targets multi-layer LC photonic structures: stacked
waveplates with independently addressed twist profiles, and a proof of
concept broadband achromatic lens. Risk mitigation includes an alternative
photonic-resin route should mesogen shrinkage prove limiting.
